results.title: Fuelfinance: Cloud-based financial department for startups | Product Hunt
results.content:
4.9

Based on 14 reviews

Review Fuelfinance?

Reviewers describe Fuelfinance as a strong hands-on finance support service that helps organize company finances, rebuild financial systems, and improve visibility into P&L and cash flow. Users repeatedly praise the team’s responsiveness and ongoing support, saying the service feels like having a finance department without the overhead and frees leaders to focus on bigger priorities instead of accounting work. A few reviews are vague, and there is virtually no detailed criticism, so the clearest takeaway is consistently positive sentiment around support, organization, and day-to-day financial management.

results.title: Logseq Reviews (2026) | Product Hunt
results.content:
Based on 64 reviews

Review Logseq?

Reviewers mostly praise Logseq for fast, low-friction note capture built around daily journals, outlining, and backlinks, which makes it easy to connect ideas without heavy folder organization. Many say it works especially well for research, writing, task tracking, and personal knowledge management, with PDF annotation, queries, plugins, and Markdown-based files adding depth and portability. The main caveats are a learning curve, rougher UI/UX than some rivals, occasional lag, and uneven sync or mobile experience, especially on Android and iOS.

Logseq is a joyful, open-source outliner that works on top of local plain-text Markdown and Org-mode files. Use it to write, organize and share your thoughts, keep your to-do list, and build your own digital garden.

 Overview
 Reviews64
 Alternatives

# Logseq Reviews

### The community submitted 64 reviews to tell us what they like about Logseq, what Logseq can do better, and more.

Based on 64 reviews

Review Logseq? [...] Logseq's blend of intuitive design and powerful features is mesmerizing. The outliner-based note-taking is transformative; quickly jotting notes, connecting complex thoughts - all effortless. Bidirectional linking is a superpower, creating a web of knowledge. More superpowers come with its extensions / plugins; I recommend Bullet Threading, Matter, Tags, Tabs. Logseq's commitment to privacy stands out. Your data stays local by default, giving you complete control (however, I opted for the iCloud-Sync; works like beta-software.) The developers constantly engage with the community, tirelessly improving and expanding the platform. Hope they focus their attention on the iOS-App. Putting links etc. via the iOS-native share-feature in the app does not work reliable. I still use OneNote for that

results.title: Kodus: Open-source AI Code Review that won’t let you break prod | Product Hunt
results.content:
Pedro Maia

We’ve tested a ton of AI review tools, and most just dump generic comments. Kodus is different, it learns our architecture and applies our own rules. It caught edge cases that even senior reviewers missed. Review time dropped by 60%, and the devs actually like using it. This is the first AI code review tool that feels production-ready.

Lilou Lane

Refreshing to see a tool that actually adapts to the team’s workflow. Curious how it handles collaboration and PR comments.

Alex Cloudstar
Product Hunt Wrapped 2025

Product Hunt Wrapped 2025

Congrats on launch! Love the focus on getting safe code to prod, not just more code. Open source, choose-any-model, and policy-based reviews are spot on. Tech debt tracking and Jira or Notion context are huge. How does Kody handle monorepos? [...] That’s why we built Kody — an open-source code-review agent that learns how your team really works.  
  
Here’s how it works:  
  
🔑 Use any model you want  
Run Kody with your own API keys and choose whichever model fits best — OpenAI, Claude, Gemini, Anthropic, and more.  
  
🧠 Learns from your context  
Kody analyzes your team’s codebase, architecture, and workflow to deliver reviews aligned with how you actually build software.  
  
⚙️ Define your own rules  
Write natural-language policies or pick from hundreds of ready-made rules in our library to make sure every review follows your team’s best practices.  
  
💼 Bring business context in  
Connect tools like Jira, Notion, or Linear so Kody understands specs, tickets, and requirements while reviewing code. [...] Hey Product Hunt! 👋 Kodus team here!  
  
We launched Kodus about 10 months ago. It took us a while to come here (part nerves, part wanting to make sure we were truly solving something meaningful for engineering teams).  
  
But the time has come 🙌  
  
We believe the biggest challenge in AI isn’t generating more code — it’s ensuring that code reaches production with quality and security.  
  
To give you an idea: about 60% of AI-generated code has quality issues.  
  
Today, teams are buried under a flood of PRs, complex business rules, and increasingly strict compliance standards.  
  
The problem is, existing review tools aren’t flexible — you can’t adapt them to your team’s standards, choose your model, or customize how reviews are done.

results.title: Best way to collect reviews with small user base?
results.content:
Report

Share

1yr ago

Image 33: Kay Kwak

Kay Kwak

Adding a popup or banner to ask users for reviews sounds like a great idea. For example, a message like "Your feedback matters! Help us improve!" or an incentive offer can grab their attention. And the review process should be kept simple. Allow users to leave quick feedback while using the product, and collect their email to request more detailed reviews later via email. Hope this helps! 😊

Rajni devi

Your in-app idea is smart. I was in the same spot. I politely asked for a written review within our tool’s dashboard, offering a small thank you like a month of premium. I also realized if your product is being treated differently than your team intended, search identity discovery feedback. It’s a useful concept for closing that perception gap. For genuine reviews, direct, contextual asks work best.

Sophia Falck-Ytter

#### What's great [...] Sophia Falck-Ytter

#### What's great

We use Featurebase for support, surveys, help center, and roadmap at Filently. All four use cases work really well out of the box. The help center in particular stands out visually – clean, professional, easy to set up. On top of that, the AI chat that builds on it is genuinely strong. It handles user inquiries autonomously, even when we're not around. For a two-person team, that's a big deal.

#### What needs improvement

One gap we've hit: proactive lifecycle messaging and automation. We're adding a second tool for that. If Featurebase ever closes that gap, it becomes a true all-in-one for early-stage teams.

#### vs Alternatives

Crisp [...] #### vs Alternatives

Crisp

We evaluated Crisp as the main alternative. Crisp is a stronger pure support tool, but Featurebase won because of its product focus: the public roadmap, feature voting, and changelog are not just support features. They're marketing and PMF assets for an early-stage SaaS team.
